text:
Coal-to-olefins has been said to reduce China's reliance on oil to keep the petchem industry running.

My very rudimentary way of explaining it:

The heart of the petrochemical industry is naphtha, which is produced from the CDU. You then use this to put into a steam cracker and produce ethylene, propylene and a bunch of other cracked products. These are all called "Olefins". These is then used into the downstream petchem derivatives industry to make all sorts of plastics.

There is another technology where you take COAL and make ethylene and propylene out of it. This is where the term Coal-to-olefins (CTO) is born. Essentially you bypass the steam cracker entirely. Presto! No oil needed to manufacture plastics!

What China then did with the freed up naphtha is to divert it into mogas blending instead to support the transportation demand. Tada! Oil demand reduced!

Not sure how structural this would be, but if the econs make sense and the government doesn't disapprove of CTO, why won't it be? China also brought online several steam crackers that takes ethane as feedstock. Yet another clever way to diversify out of naphtha.

Of course, the rest of Asia does not have this flexibility. So naphtha (and LPG) still would be required to keep the rest of their petchem units running.

BRENT’s six-month calendar spread has slumped into a small contango for the first time since October 2025 and before that December 2023. The surge of oil exported through the re-opened Strait of Hormuz has created a temporary oversupply in the spot market. The six-month spread is currently trading in a contango of around 18 cents per barrel down from a backwardation of as much as $37 at the end of March when the fighting between the United States and Iran was at its most intense:

Strait of Hormuz over the last 24 hours.

US escorts continue in the Oman lane, but the pace of the inbound tankers (crude) has not increased.

It is interesting to see more tankers using the Iranian route, but most are carrying products. 

Because of the rush of outflows, Iran does not have enough VLCCs to take crude away. I suspect they are using some of the tankers going to Iraq to transport some of the crude there. This is why if you see some of the tankers going to Iraq to load crude, I would be skeptical to assume that they are there just for Iraq.

According to data from @phileeppos , the pace of the outflow has slowed meaningfully since the escalation the week prior. We still have a gap of ~4 to ~5 million b/d even accounting for bypass. More and more of the outbound crude is headed for China, so they are coming back. It will only be obvious to the market after the fact.

Just to add a few thoughts—while I’m still cautious about calling a fundamental bottom in gold, Thursday’s NFP print forced the market to scale back its hyper-aggressive tightening bets.

We could certainly dissect the finer details of the NFP data, like the participation rate, but the tape tends to fixate on the headline payroll figure. The 2s10s curve had been on a relentless flattening path recently, but it shifted into a bull steepening regime right after the report dropped.

The market had tilted so sharply into the 'hawkish Warsh' narrative the short-rate space and the USD became heavily overcrowded. I suspect the upcoming data won't back up those extreme bets, so I’m anticipating a gradual unwinding.

As that unwind plays out, gold, PGMs, and potentially BTC could catch a significant bid. For gold to kick off a clean, beautiful rally, it really needs to be fueled by a USD rollover.

The absolute best case scenario would be the DXY breaking below 100 and building downside momentum. That said i’m fully aware of the risk that if easing US growth expectations coincide with a rollback of tightening bets from foreign central banks like the ECB, the greenback might not flush as hard as people think.

That’s exactly why I’m keeping my guard up. While it would be ideal if the market just rallies from here without giving the weak hands a chance to get back in, I’m holding onto a massive cash cushion to buffer against the risk of another flush.

Because I don't view this as a definitive floor, retail investors trying to play gold via leveraged ETFs are likely setting themselves up for a world of psychological and financial pain.

Frankly, anyone trading 3x leveraged gold or gold miners is out of their mind. In a space this vol, it blows right past what a retail account can realistically survive, meaning they'll likely get stopped out or capitulate long before we ever reach the final destination.

The right playbook here is either using derivatives to build a structured expression or slowly accumulating low-leverage cash equities. At the very least, the r/r ratio has cleared into an highly attractive zone.

As I mentioned in my previous post, outside of gold, my eyes are firmly on Japanese equities. While I'm strictly trading the Nikkei index on a tactical, fast-money basis, I used the June flush to scoop up a few Japanese humanoid names for the core portfolio and plan to sit on them for a while.

What appeals to an old school investor like me is the Japanese humanoid space actually features companies with real numbers printed on their balance sheets.

While humanoids are my high conviction favorite, I’m looking to expand into other sectors within Japan. I haven't spotted the right entry points just yet, so I guess I’ll just pray for a fat finger error flashes the Nikkei down 30% lmao.

The crypto market is rebounding: BTC needs $67K to reverse course

Market Overview

The crypto market is continuing its recovery, which coincided with the start of the calendar month. Market capitalisation has reached $2.14T, up from a low of $2.02T at the start of 1 July. Buying is taking place across the board. Amongst the individual performers over the last 24 hours, the leaders were Uniswap (+13.1%), SushiSwap (+6.8%) and Cardano (+6.5%), while Hedera (-1.9%) and IOTA (-1.7%) lost ground. The crypto market is edging up from below its 200-week moving average. However, we stand by our view that a dip below, or even an approach to, this line reflects a bearish sentiment in the cryptocurrency market, which often lasts for weeks or months. On the other hand, we have not seen any significant historical falls below this curve, so it would be overly pessimistic, in our view, to expect a pullback to $1T, the starting point of the last bull market in 2023.

Bitcoin rose above $62K at one point on Thursday but retreated to $61.6K amid low trading activity due to the US weekend. On daily timeframes, the RSI has moved out of oversold territory and formed a bullish divergence with the price, indicating that selling momentum is waning and laying the groundwork for a rebound. However, it would only be reasonable to speak of a fundamental reversal of the downtrend if the price consolidates above the $67–68K range, where the local highs from June, the 50-day moving average and the 61.8% retracement level of the May–June decline are concentrated.

News Background

CryptoQuant points to a growing proportion of investors in the red. Losses are reaching record levels, suggesting that the bottom is near.

The collapse in Strategy shares, although painful, is a necessary part of the market cycle, signalling that Bitcoin is approaching its bottom, said Matt Hougan, Chief Investment Officer at Bitwise. In his view, a new BTC bull market could begin this autumn.

Japanese investment firm Metaplanet purchased a further 2,823 bitcoins in the second quarter, according to its quarterly report. The company’s total holdings have reached 43,000 BTC, making it the third-largest corporate holder of the leading cryptocurrency.

According to Arkham Intelligence, brothers Cameron and Tyler Winklevoss transferred Bitcoin and Ethereum totalling around $67 million to hot wallets on their crypto exchange, Gemini. Previously, such transactions were followed by cryptocurrency sales.

Summary: The crypto market is recovering across the board, but for BTC to confirm a reversal, it needs to consolidate above $67–68K. (Media Man Peg On): Just keep enjoying the rollercoast. If it stops being fun, it's time to get out. Or, enjoy the journey!

The dollar: has the trend been broken?

The fall in the USD index could mark the beginning of the end

Rumours of currency interventions sent USDJPY tumbling

The US dollar plummeted to a two-week low amid disappointing labour market figures. Non-farm payrolls rose by 57,000 in June and were revised downwards by 74,000 for April and May. The fall in the unemployment rate to 4.2% was due to a reduction in the labour force rather than an acceleration in hiring.

Against the backdrop of these figures, the probability of a rate rise in July fell from 30% to 20% in the markets; the probability of a rise by September fell from 64% to 53%; and the probability of a rise by the end of the year fell from 83% to 78%. This put pressure on the dollar. Earlier, speculators had increased their net long positions in the US currency to a one-and-a-half-year high, making this trade heavily concentrated. Profit-taking became the catalyst for the rise in EURUSD.

According to Credit Agricole, the US dollar appears overbought and overvalued. The Fed may, in fact, not be as ‘hawkish’ as the markets believe. Eurizon SLJ Capital maintains that investors have already squeezed everything they can out of the USD index rally. The positive sentiment has been fully priced in; it is now time to take profits.

TD Securities argues that as global GDP accelerates and risk premiums narrow, the interest rate differential between the US and the rest of the world will narrow, leading to a weakening of the greenback in the second half of the year. The stabilisation of the international situation following turmoil caused by tariffs and the conflict in the Middle East could also trigger a depreciation of the dollar’s exchange rate.

Meanwhile, the sharpest fall in USDJPY since Japan’s currency interventions in April and May has prompted speculation about whether the authorities have resumed such practices. Was there intervention in the forex market, or did speculators’ fears prompt them to close out the short positions of their own accord?

A Reuters report and a speech by Atsushi Mimura triggered the sell-off in USDJPY. The news agency claims that the government has adopted a new tactic: rather than giving a warning of currency interventions, as it did at the end of April, it will now rely on the element of surprise. The chief currency official, the Vice Minister of Finance for International Affairs, stated that the previous intervention was justified, and that the US does not object to, but rather supports, such actions.

Summary: Weak US economic data has increased pressure on the dollar, whilst rumours of possible intervention by Japan have triggered a sharp fall in USDJPY. (FxPro)

Brent: surplus spooks the bulls

• Goldman Sachs expects an oil market surplus of 3 million BPD in 2027.

• Without Warsh’s help, Japan will be forced to resort to currency interventions.

The Dollar is consolidating ahead of Kevin Warsh’s speech in Sintra, Portugal, and the release of key US labour market data. A hawkish surprise from the new Fed Chair at the June FOMC meeting sent financial markets into a tailspin, leading to a rise in Treasury yields and the greenback. If the central bank chief hints that he was misunderstood, everything will be turned on its head.

Meanwhile, the strengthening of the dollar has been one of the reasons behind the yen’s fall to 40-year lows and Brent’s rapid return to pre-Middle East conflict levels. In the spring, the rise in oil prices drove the dollar higher, but now the strengthening dollar is putting downward pressure on oil prices. That said, there are already plenty of headwinds for ‘black gold’.

Since the deal with the US was concluded, Iran has exported around 40 million barrels. Coupled with the rise in Russian oil supplies to 4.13M BPD, the highest since the beginning of 2022, this is exerting significant downward pressure on Brent.

Despite flare-ups in the conflict in the Middle East, tanker traffic through the Strait of Hormuz continues. The resumption of operations there allows Goldman Sachs to forecast a 3M BPD surplus in the oil market by 2027. Of this, 1 million BPD will be used to replenish strategic reserves. However, the remaining figure will be sufficient to give crude oil bears confidence.

The only thing likely to unsettle them would be the resumption of large-scale US air strikes against Iran. Donald Trump has not ruled out such action, but favours diplomacy.

Meanwhile, the USDJPY has exceeded 162 for the first time since 1986. Investors are bracing for a resumption of currency interventions, on which Japan spent over $70 billion in April and May. According to Finance Minister Satsuki Katayama, the government and the central bank are ready to take appropriate measures regarding currencies at any time as necessary. The official noted that her US counterpart, Scott Bessent, had agreed with this.

If Kevin Warsh’s speech in Sintra fails to halt the US dollar’s advance, Japan will have no choice but to intervene in the forex market.

Summary: A strong dollar and rising supply are weighing on Brent; Japan is preparing to intervene amid a fall in the yen. (FxPro)

Bitcoin at $59K: Strategy’s sell-off fears are weighing on the market

Market Overview

The crypto market is trading at $2.06T, unchanged from the previous day. Still, the current levels represent a recovery from a low of $2.02T, where the cap had fallen at the start of the active US trading session and again at the opening of the Asian session. Cryptocurrencies remain under pressure from fears of selling by Strategy, casting a shadow over the relatively positive backdrop of favourable stock market dynamics and a declining dollar. Among the top coins over the past 24 hours, the leaders were Stellar (+7%), Cardano (+4.8%) and Bitcoin Cash (+3.6%). The underperformers were Aave (-3.9%), Algorand (-3.2%) and Basic Attention Token (-3%).

The sentiment index plummeted to 11, its lowest level in roughly the last three weeks. June turned out to be a rather gloomy month, as sentiment was above the ‘extreme fear’ threshold of 25 on only the 1st, remaining within the range of 8 to 23 for the rest of the month.

At the start of the day on 1 July, Bitcoin slipped to $57.8K, having recovered to above $59K at the time of writing. At its lowest point, BTC hit its lowest level since September 2024, and the subsequent attempt to rebound still looks rather tentative. The leading cryptocurrency is gradually slipping below the 200-week moving average, which is typical behaviour for it at this point on the curve, judging by several past instances.

Bitcoin fell by 20.5% in June to $58.6K, marking its second consecutive month of decline following a two-month rally. This monthly decline was the steepest in the last four years. From a seasonal perspective, July is generally considered a fairly successful month for BTC. Over the past 15 years, Bitcoin has ended the month higher on ten occasions and lower on five. The average gain was 19%, while the average decline was 7.8%.

News Background

Over the past week, BitMine purchased an additional 27,084 ETH worth $43 million, bringing the total amount of Ethereum in its reserves to 5.7 million ETH, which exceeds 4.7% of the total Ethereum supply. BitMine has achieved 94% of its target to consolidate 5% of the second-largest cryptocurrency's total supply over the past 11 months, according to BitMine's Chairman, Tom Lee.

According to Arkham Intelligence, the US company Sharplink has resumed buying Ethereum after an eight-month hiatus. Last week, the company acquired 39,196 Ether for $62.4 million.

Strategy did not buy any Bitcoin last week, but sold a block of MSTR ordinary shares, increasing its dollar reserves to $2.55 billion. According to the company, these funds will be sufficient to cover 17 months of expected share-based payments.

Following the latest adjustment, the Bitcoin mining difficulty rose sharply by 7.15% to 133.87 T. This is the largest increase in the metric since February.

According to SoSoValue, investors withdrew $4.3B from US Bitcoin ETFs in June. This monthly outflow was the largest since the launch of the Bitcoin ETF in January 2024.

Summary: The crypto market is at lows, with sentiment in the ‘fear’ zone. BTC fell by 20% in June and is trading around $59K, under pressure from selling and ETF outflows, despite a localised recovery. (FxPro)

June 30

Oil

Oil finds no silver lining

Oil has returned to the levels seen before the armed conflict in the Middle East much sooner than expected. Brent’s fall of almost 40% from its March highs is not solely due to more barrels coming from the Persian Gulf. The market has adapted to the most far-reaching crisis in the history of the oil market. As a result, traffic through the Strait of Hormuz need not return to previous levels to restore balance. A return to 65%, or 11-12M BPD, will be sufficient.

Morgan Stanley put forward this assumption and lowered its forecast for the average Brent price to $75 per barrel in the third and fourth quarters, a $15 and $5 lower than its previous estimate, respectively. According to the bank, while more barrels are flowing through the main oil artery, the bears’ previous trump cards remain in play. These include record growth in US exports and a decline in Chinese energy imports.

The Gulf states’ search for alternative routes has made it possible to partially meet demand from Europe and Asia for the next couple of months. These continents do not require any more oil. The Iranian output could also surge to 3.3M BPD by the end of the year, following the lifting of sanctions. Combined with the gradual recovery in regional production, this creates a distinctly bearish backdrop for Brent.

There is no doubt that the market will stabilise over the coming weeks. It will need to replenish global stocks, which have been considerably depleted, and meet growing demand from Europe and Asia. Oil prices could even rise if the conflict in the Middle East escalates. If the conflict does not escalate further, the long-term outlook for Brent looks rather bleak.

According to estimates from the International Energy Agency, global crude oil supply in 2026 will fall by 3.9M BPD in 2026. Supply is expected to increase by 8M to 110.3M BPD in 2027. Demand will recover at a much more modest pace, and next year the oil market risks facing a surplus of 5M BPD.

Thus, barring an escalation of the geopolitical conflict in the Middle East, the downward trend in Brent is likely to persist.

Summary: Brent has returned to pre-crisis levels, whilst rising supplies and weak demand reinforce bearish sentiment. Barring a new escalation, the decline is likely to continue.

$4,000: Just a pause, not the end of Gold's decline

Gold is rebounding at the end of the week after a correction in the odds of two key Fed rate hikes this year. Nevertheless, the price is falling for the fourth consecutive week. Since the second half of the week, bears have been relentlessly trying to push the price below the psychologically significant $4,000 mark but eased their grip on Friday afternoon. At the same time, the long-term moving averages favour sellers, as a ‘death cross’ is looming (when the 50-day moving average falls below the 200-day moving average), reinforced by the fact that the price is trading below this cross. On the weekly charts, attempts to push the price back above the 50-week moving average have failed. However, this is also the price support zone from the end of last year, so we can expect a fairly fierce battle around the current level.

It is also worth noting how the narrative for gold’s price movements has changed. Previously, the escalation between the US and Iran triggered downward price movements, with a clear inverse correlation between oil and gold. But it has now become apparent that both assets can move in the same direction – downwards.

However, we must also consider an alternative scenario, in which, since the end of January, the price of gold has been correcting following three years of growth, reaching the 61.8% level—a classic Fibonacci support level. Under this scenario, we should see consolidation followed by a reversal to the upside, with the first stage being a recovery to peak levels around $5,600, and the second stage a rally to $8,000 as part of an extension to the 161.8% level. We nevertheless view this undoubtedly inspiring scenario as less likely, given the history of previous similar price booms and crashes over the last half-century. (FxPro)

June 30

Oil

Oil finds no silver lining

Oil has returned to the levels seen before the armed conflict in the Middle East much sooner than expected. Brent’s fall of almost 40% from its March highs is not solely due to more barrels coming from the Persian Gulf. The market has adapted to the most far-reaching crisis in the history of the oil market. As a result, traffic through the Strait of Hormuz need not return to previous levels to restore balance. A return to 65%, or 11-12M BPD, will be sufficient.

Morgan Stanley put forward this assumption and lowered its forecast for the average Brent price to $75 per barrel in the third and fourth quarters, a $15 and $5 lower than its previous estimate, respectively. According to the bank, while more barrels are flowing through the main oil artery, the bears’ previous trump cards remain in play. These include record growth in US exports and a decline in Chinese energy imports.

The Gulf states’ search for alternative routes has made it possible to partially meet demand from Europe and Asia for the next couple of months. These continents do not require any more oil. The Iranian output could also surge to 3.3M BPD by the end of the year, following the lifting of sanctions. Combined with the gradual recovery in regional production, this creates a distinctly bearish backdrop for Brent.

There is no doubt that the market will stabilise over the coming weeks. It will need to replenish global stocks, which have been considerably depleted, and meet growing demand from Europe and Asia. Oil prices could even rise if the conflict in the Middle East escalates. If the conflict does not escalate further, the long-term outlook for Brent looks rather bleak.

According to estimates from the International Energy Agency, global crude oil supply in 2026 will fall by 3.9M BPD in 2026. Supply is expected to increase by 8M to 110.3M BPD in 2027. Demand will recover at a much more modest pace, and next year the oil market risks facing a surplus of 5M BPD.

Thus, barring an escalation of the geopolitical conflict in the Middle East, the downward trend in Brent is likely to persist.

Summary: Brent has returned to pre-crisis levels, whilst rising supplies and weak demand reinforce bearish sentiment. Barring a new escalation, the decline is likely to continue.

However, this is also the price support zone from the end of last year, so we can expect a fairly fierce battle around the current level.

It is also worth noting how the narrative for gold’s price movements has changed. Previously, the escalation between the US and Iran triggered downward price movements, with a clear inverse correlation between oil and gold. But it has now become apparent that both assets can move in the same direction – downwards.

However, we must also consider an alternative scenario, in which, since the end of January, the price of gold has been correcting following three years of growth, reaching the 61.8% level—a classic Fibonacci support level. Under this scenario, we should see consolidation followed by a reversal to the upside, with the first stage being a recovery to peak levels around $5,600, and the second stage a rally to $8,000 as part of an extension to the 161.8% level. 押注霍尔木兹航运恢复，机构预测布油年底最低或跌至60美元

财联社7月4日讯（编辑 赵昊）随着全球石油市场有望重新转向供过于求，一些华尔街策略师开始认为布油价格到今年年底可能跌至每桶60美元，这一水平相当于较中东战事期间的峰值腰斩。

花旗集团分析师在周五的一份报告中表示，尽管局势可能出现短暂波动，但美伊双方都有充分理由维持6月中旬签署的谅解备忘录，石油供应增加将在今年下半年对油价形成下行压力。

花旗建议交易员在今夏油价反弹时逢高卖出，并预测到今年年底，布伦特原油价格将在每桶60至65美元之间。

美伊签署谅解备忘录至今已有两周，但双方围绕备忘录的具体条款和执行依然争论不休

对此，花旗分析师写道，“我们预计谅解备忘录将得到维持，并不是因为双方突然建立了信任，而是因为打破协议对双方都缺乏足够的激励。”

“更广泛的政治背景表明，局势将趋于稳定并最终达成协议，而不是重新走向对抗。”

他们还指出，石油市场的供需基本面仍然“结构性疲软”，“如果产油国决定加快已启动的增产计划，市场供需平衡可能迅速转向明显过剩”。

布伦特原油自2月底以来大幅上涨，随着霍尔木兹海峡被迫关闭航运后进一步飙升。4月30日，布油一度突破每桶126美元，创下2022年以来最高水平。

但随着中东冲突缓和，以及6月美伊签署谅解备忘录后，油价持续回落。截至发稿，布伦特原油报每桶72.13美元。

需要指出的是，花旗预测的油价明显低于市场普遍预期。目前，华尔街对2026年底布油价格的预测中值为78美元，而布油2027年1月交割合约报73美元附近。

上月，高盛将其年底布油价格预测下调至每桶80美元。该行近期报告指出，海湾地区石油运输迅速恢复，80美元的预测基于霍尔木兹海峡的航运流量将在7月底前恢复至战前水平。

高盛也表示，在更乐观的供应正常化情景下，布伦特原油价格到2026年底可能降至每桶60美元左右。

不过，这一预测的前提包括：海湾地区石油出口在“7月初”恢复至战前水平、全球原油日产量比预期高出100万桶以上，以及全球需求弱于预期。

花旗分析师称，目前通过霍尔木兹海峡的原油运输量已回升至每日700万桶。与之相比，冲突爆发前约为每日1500万桶。

他们还指出，实际运输量可能高于官方统计数据，因为出于安全考虑，许多油轮关闭了船舶应答器，因此未被完整纳入统计。

Canada’s Trade Dependence on America Endures Despite Diversification Rhetoric

4th July 2026

Recent months have delivered a revealing paradox for Canadian policymakers. Trade surpluses have widened, energy export revenues have surged, and federal coffers have received an unexpected lift from sharply higher global oil prices. The driver was not domestic reform or new market access but geopolitical turmoil in the Middle East, particularly the conflict involving Iran and disruptions around the Strait of Hormuz. Producers and resource-rich provinces have recorded windfall gains. Yet the same government that celebrates these figures continues to impose regulatory and policy constraints that limit the sector’s capacity to expand output or lock in long-term investment. This tension between short-term price-driven gains and structural self-limitation runs through much of Ottawa’s current approach to trade and economic management.

The pattern is not confined to energy. It appears in claims of successful diversification away from the United States, in the presentation of incremental partnerships as transformative deals, and in the accounting treatment of defence spending targets. Across these areas, measurable outcomes for Canadian citizens, productivity, affordability, investment certainty and real per-capita growth, lag behind the narrative of progress. Geography, integrated supply chains and basic economic gravity continue to assert themselves more forcefully than diplomatic initiatives or administrative reclassifications.

The Enduring Continental Anchor

Canada’s merchandise exports to the United States still accounted for approximately 72 per cent on a full-year 2025 basis, down only modestly from 76 per cent the year before. Monthly figures have occasionally fallen toward 67 per cent or slightly lower, yet the absolute scale of trade, the depth of cross-border production networks and the irreplaceable nature of market access remain unchanged. Energy and automotive sectors dominate the flows. These are not easily substituted with smaller or more distant partners, however numerous the agreements signed with them.

The United States remains, by a wide margin, Canada’s largest trading partner in both goods and services. Integrated supply chains in vehicles and parts, energy infrastructure and just-in-time manufacturing create mutual dependencies that predate any current government and will outlast it. When energy exports are excluded, the United States typically runs a surplus with Canada. When they are included, Canada records a surplus, but one heavily dependent on price movements and on the reliability of continental infrastructure rather than on new diversified outlets.

This reality sets hard limits on what diversification strategies can achieve. Pre-existing agreements such as the Comprehensive Economic and Trade Agreement with the European Union and the Comprehensive and Progressive Agreement for Trans-Pacific Partnership already provide substantial non-US coverage. Newer initiatives under the current government have added strategic partnerships, memoranda of understanding and early-stage dialogues with countries including Indonesia, the Philippines, India and members of ASEAN and Mercosur. These instruments are useful for targeted sectors and relationship-building. They do not, however, replicate the scale, depth or immediacy of the US market, nor do they overcome the physical and logistical advantages of proximity.

The CUSMA Review and Transactional Realities

The Canada, United States, Mexico Agreement, which replaced NAFTA and entered into force in 2020, contains a mandatory six-year joint review that began on 1 July 2026. The agreement runs for an initial 16-year term ending in 2036. If the parties do not confirm extension in writing, it moves to annual reviews, introducing sustained uncertainty for roughly 1.3 trillion in annual bilateral trade.

According to the German budget project, which Reuters has reviewed, Germany plans to increase borrowing in 2027 to more than 203 billion euros, "as Berlin is increasing investments and defense spending to support its economy after several years of insufficient investment" and energy shocks

The project raises the total expenditure of the German budget in 2027 to 555.4 billion euros, which was achieved thanks to new fiscal rules that exempt defense spending from debt restrictions.

The main defense expenditure is expected to increase from 82 billion euros in 2026 to 109 billion euros in 2027, reaching approximately 130.1 billion euros including funding for Ukraine.

The budget annotation contains an interesting warning that if disruptions in the Strait of Hormuz or in regional oil production continue after the summer, this could have "far-reaching consequences" for the German economy. Despite this, it is expected that the budget project will be approved by the Cabinet of Ministers on Monday and then presented to Parliament before the end of this year.

Dangote Refinery Clarifies: Pricing Not Tied to Daily International Crude Oil Quotations

In its official statement, the refinery clarified that its pricing does not move in tandem with daily international crude oil quotations. Added that Crude oil is procured weeks and in some cases months before it is processed, under commercial contracts linked primarily to monthly average pricing mechanisms rather than prevailing spot market prices. 

The statement reads: “Dangote Petroleum Refinery & Petrochemicals remains committed to ensuring that Nigerians benefit from favourable market developments through fair, responsible and sustainable pricing of petroleum products.
Since May 30, 2026, Dangote Petroleum Refinery has reduced the ex-depot price of Premium Motor Spirit (PMS) by N200 per litre, Automotive Gas Oil (AGO) by N300 per litre and Jet A1 aviation fuel by N520 per litre, despite continuing to process crude oil acquired during a period when international crude prices were significantly higher than current levels.

“These reductions demonstrate our commitment to passing on cost efficiencies to consumers while maintaining the operational and financial sustainability of domestic refining.

“It is important to clarify that refinery pricing does not move in tandem with daily international crude oil quotations. Crude oil is procured weeks, and in some cases months, before it is processed, under commercial contracts linked primarily to monthly average pricing mechanisms rather than prevailing spot market prices.

“Consequently, the petroleum products currently being supplied from our refinery are being produced from crude inventories acquired at substantially higher costs than today's market prices. The average landed cost of crude processed by the refinery was approximately US$124.80 per barrel in May and US$95.25 per barrel in June, compared with the current international benchmark of about US$71.01 per barrel.

“Furthermore, refinery feedstock is not purchased at the headline ICE Brent price commonly reported in the media. Our crude is acquired on a Dated Brent plus market premium, freight and logistics cost basis, resulting in actual landed costs that differ materially from benchmark quotations.

“Notwithstanding these elevated feedstock costs, Dangote Petroleum Refinery did not immediately transfer the full impact of rising crude prices to the Nigerian market. Instead, the refinery absorbed a substantial portion of the increase in order to support market stability, reduce inflationary pressures and shield consumers from the extreme volatility witnessed in global energy markets. For this reason, prices of petroleum products in Nigeria are still lower than prices in neighbouring countries even after adjusting for taxes.

“As lower cost crude cargoes progressively enter our production cycle, we have commenced a phased reduction in product prices.

“Today's N50 per litre reduction is the fourth price cut in one month, bringing cumulative reductions to above N200 per litre on PMS. This approach ensures that pricing decisions are anchored on actual production economics and inventory costs rather than short term fluctuations in international oil markets.

“Nigeria today benefits from the stabilising role of domestic refining capacity. Five regions. Offline simultaneously.

🇺🇸 US EAST COAST — Monroe Energy (Trainer, PA) dark since June 25. No restart timeline. Delta’s 185 kb/d refinery — supplying ~75% of the airline’s total jet fuel via pipeline to NY and Atlanta — taken out by a pump room fire 6 days ago. Monroe Energy going dark June 25 threatens that lifeline at exactly the wrong moment — US summer driving season is already pulling refinery yields toward gasoline and away from jet. Every barrel the US can’t export to Europe is a barrel Europe can’t replace.

Post 1. 

Mark Carney: The Emperor with No Clothes

4th July 2026

Mark Carney strode into the role of Prime Minister in 2025 as the ultimate credentialed insider: Harvard and Oxford economist, former Governor of the Bank of Canada and Bank of England, UN Special Envoy for Climate Action and Finance, and vice-chair at Brookfield Asset Management steering transition investing. His polished persona, global network, and technocratic fluency promised competent stewardship in a turbulent world of US tariffs, energy transitions, and domestic strains.

Yet more than a year into his mandate, the emperor’s wardrobe is looking threadbare. Carney’s government has delivered incremental announcements dressed as bold progress, while core Canadian challenges, layered regulation, ballooning public debt, stagnant productivity, housing unaffordability, and constrained resource development, deepen under a familiar pattern of administrative expansion and narrative management rather than root reform.

The Pipeline Paradox: Symbolism Over Substance

Nowhere is this disconnect clearer than in energy policy. In early July 2026, Carney stood with Alberta Premier Danielle Smith to announce the formal submission of a proposed southern Alberta-to-Vancouver pipeline route (the West Coast Pipeline Project) to Ottawa’s Major Projects Office. The plan envisions up to one million barrels per day, following much of the existing Trans Mountain corridor, with backing from major pipeline firms. It came after a 2025 federal-Alberta memorandum of understanding and was paired with a multi-billion-dollar infrastructure deal with British Columbia Premier David Eby that included funding for projects like the George Massey Tunnel replacement.

On paper, it looks like momentum for tidewater access and export diversification beyond the US. In reality, it exemplifies managed continuity. The northern BC tanker ban, rooted in 1970s concerns over tanker traffic and formalised in the 2019 Oil Tanker Moratorium Act (Bill C-48), was explicitly reaffirmed as “consistent” and non-negotiable. Modern double-hull tankers, advanced navigation, and spill-prevention data have transformed risk profiles since the Exxon Valdez era, yet the 1970s-era restriction persists, channelling any new capacity southward.

Progress on the southern route comes tethered to emissions-reduction conditions, methane rules, carbon pricing alignment, and BC’s DRIPA consent requirements, frameworks that empower additional veto points and can delay or derail projects. Billions in stalled or cancelled energy initiatives over the past decade illustrate the cumulative cost of such layered constraints. Alberta gains procedural clarity on one corridor; BC secures offsets and reassurance on its northern coast; the broader national interest in reliable, high-capacity export infrastructure and fiscal resilience receives only conditional, incremental support.

This is not the decisive unleashing of Canada’s resource endowment that evidence-based policy would suggest. It is the preservation of older restrictions alongside new performance obligations, precisely the administrative layering that has long deterred investment while insulating decision-makers from accountability.

Debt, Productivity, and the Expanding State

The fiscal picture reinforces the pattern. Canada’s combined federal-provincial net debt is projected to surpass $2.4 trillion in 2025/26, having nearly doubled (inflation-adjusted) since 2007/08. The debt-to-GDP ratio has climbed from around 53% to over 75%. Deficits remain elevated, with the 2025/26 federal shortfall reaching tens of billions despite revenue measures and spending restraint rhetoric.

Post 1. 

Canada’s Pipeline Paradox: Carney’s Conditional Advance Masks Enduring Bans and Consent Barriers

4th July 2026 

Prime Minister Mark Carney’s July 2026 announcements with Alberta and British Columbia premiers created the appearance of momentum on a new West Coast oil pipeline. A southern-route proposal from Bruderheim, Alberta, to the Vancouver area was referred to the Major Projects Office. This followed the November 2025 Canada-Alberta Memorandum of Understanding that set timelines for a new pipeline submission, potential national-interest designation, and parallel emissions-reduction steps.

Yet the same period saw explicit reaffirmation of the northern British Columbia tanker ban as consistent with the framework, alongside provincial policies that introduce consent requirements capable of delaying or blocking major projects.

The result is managed continuity rather than decisive capacity expansion. A pipeline is advanced on paper while older restrictions rooted in 1970s concerns and newer consent mechanisms remain in place. These combine to channel development into narrower paths subject to layered conditions, with measurable consequences for investment, export diversification and provincial revenues.

The Tanker Ban’s Origins and Technological Progress

The northern BC tanker moratorium dates to the early 1970s. Concerns over proposed tanker traffic from Alaska’s Trans-Alaska Pipeline System terminal at Valdez prompted Canadian action to keep large crude carriers away from sensitive coastal waters, including Dixon Entrance, Hecate Strait and Queen Charlotte Sound. A de facto ban emerged around 1972. It was later formalised in the 2019 Oil Tanker Moratorium Act, which prohibits tankers carrying more than 12,500 tonnes of crude oil and certain other oils from loading, unloading or stopping in those northern zones.

The 1989 Exxon Valdez spill in Alaska’s Prince William Sound reinforced global caution about tanker risks in confined or ecologically sensitive waters. In response, the United States enacted the Oil Pollution Act of 1990, which mandated double-hull construction for tankers operating in US waters. By 2015, single-hull vessels had been phased out.

Double-hull designs create a protective outer layer that absorbs impact in collisions or groundings, significantly limiting oil outflow even if the inner hull is breached. Complementary advances include smaller segregated cargo tanks, enhanced navigation and collision-avoidance systems, improved spill-containment technologies and rigorous training standards. Industry data document substantial reductions in large spill incidents and volumes attributable to these changes.

US-flagged tankers continue to transport crude from Valdez southward to West Coast refineries. These vessels generally follow established offshore routes well clear of the specific confined northern BC waters covered by the Canadian ban, often 50–100 nautical miles or more offshore or in international waters while respecting applicable exclusion zones.

The ban applies to stopping, loading or unloading in the designated zones regardless of flag, but does not halt all transit in adjacent waters.

In this light, the ban’s ongoing operation functions less as an optimised response to current technological realities and more as a policy that restricts new northern export infrastructure. Modern double-hull and safety standards have demonstrably lowered risks compared with the era that shaped the original moratorium. Retaining the restriction as a prerequisite or consistent element of pipeline planning channels Canadian ambitions toward a southern corridor while preserving the ban’s core limiting effect.

Critical minerals are often more concentrated than oil

The energy transition does not remove geopolitics. It moves it from oil fields to mines, refineries, and processing chains.

This chart lists leading producers for several important minerals. The Democratic Republic of Congo accounts for about 75.9% of cobalt mine production, Indonesia about 59.5% of nickel, China about 69.2% of rare earths, Kazakhstan about 38.7% of uranium, and Australia about 36.7% of lithium. Those shares make several supply chains look more concentrated than oil.

■ Mining is only the first layer

Cobalt:DR Congo about 75.9%

Nickel:Indonesia about 59.5%

Rare earths:China about 69.2%

Uranium:Kazakhstan about 38.7%

Lithium:Australia about 36.7%

The key issue is not only where the ore is mined, but who can turn it into usable materials. The IEA has repeatedly warned that processing and refining are often even more concentrated than mining. Rare earths are the clearest example: a country can mine the ore without controlling separation, magnets, and downstream manufacturing.

This should not be simplified into "one country fully controls one mineral." The chart shows mine production share, not reserves, refining, inventories, recycling, or substitutes. Cobalt is often a by-product of copper and nickel, which limits supply flexibility. Rare earth bottlenecks often sit in separation and magnet-making. Each mineral has a different risk profile.

The industrial-policy lesson is that the energy transition raises dependence on mineral supply chains, but the answer is not just buying mines. It is diversified production, refining capacity, recycling, substitution, long-term contracts, and strategic inventories. The next layer of energy security is not only OPEC. It is Congo, Indonesia, China, Australia, Kazakhstan, and the countries that control midstream processing.

Source: USGS Mineral Commodity Summaries, IEA Global Critical Minerals Outlook, World Nuclear Association (Image: @jackprandelli)
